claim: Agents are a separate user class from humans; they have different cognitive bottlenecks (efficiency vs. visual complexity) requiring different interface designs
actionable: Design agent-specific interfaces with semantic summaries, structured JSON, and programmatic APIs instead of HTML/visual layouts
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: Throwing raw data (50,000 lines of JSON traces) at agents exceeds context limits and pushes them into the dump zone; semantic summaries are essential
actionable: Summarize large datasets into key metrics and semantic summaries; provide markdown with high-level insights instead of raw data; point agents at conclusions
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: Tokens per successful outcome is the key efficiency metric for agent interfaces; it reveals fuel efficiency and usability of tools
actionable: Measure tokens per successful outcome for each agent tool; compare tools within same use case; optimize interface to reduce token burn for real tasks
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: Rewriting error messages so agents can self-heal (e.g., 'Cannot navigate back, no previous page' vs vague errors) reduces retry cycles and token waste
actionable: Audit all error messages; make them actionable for agents; include what went wrong, why, and how to recover without human intervention
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: Starting with one monolithic tool (debug_webpage) fails; decomposing into 25 focused tools also fails unless tool discovery is explicitly designed
actionable: Build 25 tools with clear, specific descriptions; use skills to group related tools; hide niche tools behind parameters; optimize schema descriptions
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: 97% of MCP tool descriptions have quality smells; poor schemas hurt agent tool selection and cause wasted tool calls
actionable: Write clear purpose statements for each tool; provide usage guidelines with activation criteria; use specific metric names (LCP, INP, CLS) in descriptions
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: Autoconnect friction (require confirmation each time) is intentional; removing it opens prompt injection and security risks (lethal trifecta)
actionable: Keep friction in agent authorization flows; tier access by deployment environment (local dev, CI, full internet); use domain allowlists and mitigations
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google

claim: Tool categorization (hiding niche tools) and slim mode (3 essential tools only) trade token efficiency for capability; evaluate tradeoffs per use case
actionable: Profile typical agent workflows; expose only relevant tools per context; measure token cost vs. task success for different tool sets
source_title: Building Agent Interfaces: Lessons from Chrome DevTools (MCP) for Agents — Michael Hablich, Google
